item.title: Hasbro cancels Dungeons & Dragons game led by Respawn veterans
item.content:encoded:
Image via Dungeons & Dragons and Giant Skull

The collaboration between Wizards of the Coast (WOTC), a Hasbro subsidiary, and developer Giant Skull, a studio founded by Respawn veteran Stig Asmussen, has come to a close less than a year after the announcement.

This has been confirmed by WOTC itself, which shared the news in a statement to Bloomberg. The companies were working on an action-adventure game set in the Dungeons & Dragons universe, which, at the time of its reveal, was deemed a "definitive moment in both companies' gaming ambitions."

As it turns out, WOTC pulled out of the deal earlier this year. "We assess concepts at every stage of development," a spokesperson told Bloomberg.

Moreover, the company added that, while it decided not to pursue an early concept from Giant Skull, it retains "great respect for Stig Asmussen and his team and value our ongoing relationship."

As for Asmussen himself, he told Bloomberg that he had enjoyed working with WOTC, and that he was talking to people there and at other companies about potential new publishing deals.

Related:Embracer chairman says cost control 'does not get enough respect' after presiding over years of layoffs

"Things are good at Giant Skull," he told the publication.

"Stig and the team at Giant Skull are exactly the type of exceptionally talented creators we want to work with, and I'm so happy to be reuniting with him on this new project," president of WOTC and digital gaming at Hasbro John Hight said back in June of last year.

Asmussen's team was established in 2024, formed by several Respawn Entertainment alumni and a number of former Fortnite developers.

When asked about his decision to leave a relatively stable operation at Respawn to pursue a new studio venture, Asmussen said the following: "One of my main mottos is 'no regrets. I didn't want to be in a situation where I decided to stay put and a year or two years from now, I'd be looking over my shoulder and be like 'what about that other thing I could have done?'"

Giant Skull's game isn't the only project that Hasbro has axed this year. Back in February, a company spokesperson confirmed the closure of Atomic Arcade, the studio established by WOTC and Hasbro. Yet, it also said the studio's unnamed Snake Eyes project has not been canceled.

About the Author

Contributing Editor, News, GameDeveloper.com

Diego Nicolás Argüello is a freelance journalist and critic from Argentina. Video games helped him to learn English, so now he covers them for places like The New York Times, NPR, Rolling Stone, and more. He also runs Into the Spine, a site dedicated to fostering and supporting new writers, and co-hosted Turnabout Breakdown, a podcast about the Ace Attorney series. He’s most likely playing a rhythm game as you read this.



https://www.gamedeveloper.com/business/hasbro-cancels-dungeons-dragons-game-led-by-respawn-veteran

item.title: "Honestly? We botched it" - Kickstarter apologises for its criticised adult content rules, and for betraying its "f*ck the establishment spirit"
item.content:encoded:
"The decision we made wasn't the right one."

Crowdfunding website Kickstarter - a place many video game projects have originated - has issued an unflinching apology after 'botching' new guidelines it released last week. The changes had sought to tighten the rules around adult-oriented projects raising money there, resulting in a fierce backlash and calls of censorship.

"Honestly? We botched it," Kickster chief operating officer Sean Leow wrote in an update post. "The rules didn't land the way we intended, and the response from our community let us know loud and clear that we got it wrong."

The updated guidelines were issued because of clashes with Kickstarter's payment provider Stripe, Leow explained. Simply put, Kickstarter and Stripe have different attitudes towards mature content, and what Kickstarter deemed okay would sometimes not be seen as okay by Stripe.

This led to "a growing number" of campaigns being okayed on Kickstarter and then suspended mid-funding by Stripe. The updated rules were an attempt to "close the gap" and provide one set of rules people could work by.

"That was the intent," Leow wrote, "but the decision we made was an abandonment of the core counterculture, f*ck the establishment spirit of Kickstarter, and it left our community vulnerable.

"The decision we made wasn't the right one, and in an attempt to create rules that could work across both Kickstarter and Stripe, we rolled out something that was too restrictive and too far removed from what we actually believe."

As a result, Kickstarter has decided to scrap the newly issued mature guidelines and revert to the previous version, while it works on new guidelines for the future.

"This isn't the easiest route," Leow cautioned. It means approved campaigns can still be suspended by Stripe mid-funding - "that's the reality of operating within a payment system we don't fully control" - and even Kickstarter advocating for these campaigns might not change that outcome. To help creators avoid this, Leow provided a link to Stripe's guidelines on mature content and Kickstarter has create its own guide for dealing with mature content in order to avoid antagonising Stripe.

"Though this route is an imperfect temporary solution, it allows us to stand in what we believe in and use the space between our rules and Stripe's rules to keep fighting for creators," he added. "That means continuing to push Stripe for flexibility, clarity, and consistency, working to carve out exceptions where we can, and finding solutions that don't leave creators feeling censored before they've even launched."

Kickstarter's tussle with Stripe follows months of debate around payment providers such as MasterCard and Visa, and the pressure they exerted on gaming platforms such as Steam and Itch.io in order to more tightly control what mature games were sold there. Both platforms rolled out sweeping and somewhat vague changes as a result, causing well-intentioned adult games, including those from queer creators wishing to tell queer stories, to also get caught in the net.

Mentioned in this article



https://www.eurogamer.net/kickstarter-adult-content-rules-censorship-apology
